summary: The employee explains the differences between L4 and L7 load balancing, detailing their operational layers, capabilities, use cases, and a key downside of L7.
quality_flags: Employee's last turn is cut off mid-sentence.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: For today's software engineering interview, please explain the difference between L4 and L7 load balancing.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Sure. Let's start with Layer 4. Layer 4 load balancing operates at the transport layer of the OSI model. The load balancer only has access to things like what IP the request is coming from and what port number it's trying to access. Then it just forwards that packet to one of your servers, usually with a simple policy like least connections or round-robin.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Okay. So, it can't actually see the content of the request?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Exactly. That makes it both super fast and super secure. It can't see anything that's actually in the request.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: And when would you use this?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: L4 load balancing is great when our top priority is performance and we don't need to route requests based on the actual data. It's common for things like video streaming or video games.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: L7 load balancing happens at the application layer. The load balancer can look at things like the headers, the URL, the cookies, and even the body of the request if you want.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: And when would you use this?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: L7 load balancing is useful for things like microservices or APIs where you need to route requests to a specific server based on information beyond just the port number or IP. For example, if we get a specific URL and we know that has to go to a specific server, we need L7 load balancing.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Sounds great. Are there any downsides?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: L7 load balancing takes a lot more overhead than L4 load balancing because we have to actually open up and look at each request and then make a decision about which server it goes.

training_pairs.question: Please explain the difference between L4 and L7 load balancing.
training_pairs.answer: Sure. Let's start with Layer 4. Layer 4 load balancing operates at the transport layer of the OSI model. The load balancer only has access to things like what IP the request is coming from and what port number it's trying to access. Then it just forwards that packet to one of your servers, usually with a simple policy like least connections or round-robin.
training_pairs.follow_up: So, it can't actually see the content of the request?

training_pairs.question: And when would you use L4 load balancing?
training_pairs.answer: L4 load balancing is great when our top priority is performance and we don't need to route requests based on the actual data. It's common for things like video streaming or video games.

training_pairs.question: Can you describe L7 load balancing?
training_pairs.answer: L7 load balancing happens at the application layer. The load balancer can look at things like the headers, the URL, the cookies, and even the body of the request if you want.
training_pairs.follow_up: And when would you use this?

training_pairs.question: When would you use L7 load balancing?
training_pairs.answer: L7 load balancing is useful for things like microservices or APIs where you need to route requests to a specific server based on information beyond just the port number or IP. For example, if we get a specific URL and we know that has to go to a specific server, we need L7 load balancing.

training_pairs.question: Are there any downsides to L7 load balancing?
training_pairs.answer: L7 load balancing takes a lot more overhead than L4 load balancing because we have to actually open up and look at each request and then make a decision about which server it goes.
